1. To calculate the probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A, we need to find the probability that $X_B > X_A$. This is equivalent to finding $P(X_B > X_A)$.

Given that $X_A \sim N(\mu_A, \sigma_A^2)$ and $X_B \sim N(\mu_B, \sigma_B^2)$, we can calculate the difference $X_B - X_A$ as a new random variable. The mean of this difference is $\mu_{X_B - X_A} = \mu_B - \mu_A = 75 - 70 = 5$. The standard deviation of this difference is $\sqrt{\sigma_B^2 + \sigma_A^2} = \sqrt{12^2 + 10^2} = \sqrt{144 + 100} = \sqrt{244} \approx 15.62$.

We want to find $P(X_B > X_A)$, which is equivalent to finding $P(X_B - X_A > 0)$. This can be calculated using the standard normal distribution.

Let $Z = \frac{X_B - X_A - \mu_{X_B - X_A}}{\sigma_{X_B - X_A}} = \frac{X_B - X_A - 5}{\sqrt{244}}$. The probability we are looking for is $P(Z > 0)$.

Using the standard normal distribution table, $P(Z > 0) = 0.5$.

So the probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A is 0.5.
